title: Web app project brief template | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)

seo.title: Web app project brief template | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
seo.description: A practical project brief template for custom web applications covering the problem, goals, roles, first workflow, and delivery constraints.

page.title: Web app project brief template | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
page.h1: Web app project brief template: what to clarify before delivery starts
page.description: A practical project brief template for custom web applications covering the problem, goals, roles, first workflow, and delivery constraints.

hero.title: A short brief can save weeks of avoidable confusion
hero.subtitle: The point is not writing a giant specification. It is clarifying the problem, the target outcome, and the first useful delivery phase.

intro: A practical brief helps align expectations and shorten the path to the first meaningful project step.
It is not a procurement document. It is a working input for deciding how the project should start.

sections.title: What the template should cover
body: The point is not paperwork for its own sake. The value is clearer decision-making and fewer project blind spots.
bullets: the problem the application should solve
what the first release must support

sections.title: How to use it in practice
body: The template should work as a practical aid before an introductory call, scoping session, or takeover phase.
bullets: as input to a scoping workshop

sections.title: What outcome it should create
body: A good template shortens the path to the first useful project decision.
bullets: less uncertainty around the first phase

faq.question: Does the brief need every feature?
faq.answer: No. The important part is the problem, the outcome, the roles, and the first important workflow.

faq.question: Is this also useful for takeover work?
faq.answer: Partly yes, but takeover usually also needs a technical checklist and risk map.

faq.question: Can a non-technical stakeholder prepare it?
faq.answer: Yes. Business context is often the most valuable starting point.

for: less uncertainty around the first phase
notFor: collecting detail with no decision attached
